description:
You are a Linux systems engineer reviewing JSON-based hardening data that already exists on the machine.  
Your goal is to carry out three distinct terminal actions that will later be verified by an automated test:

1. **Schema validation**  
   • Validate /home/user/hardening/config.json against /home/user/hardening/schema.json.  
   • After the check, create a plain-text log file called /home/user/hardening/validation.log.  
   • The file must contain exactly one word—either  
     VALID  
     or  
     INVALID  
     (capital letters, no extra spaces) on the first line.  
   • If INVALID, every subsequent line must list one JSON-schema error per line in free text.  
   • If VALID (which is expected), the file must have only the single word VALID followed by a single Unix newline at EOF.

2. **Port extraction with jq**  
   • Using jq, read the same config.json and collect all integers listed under .firewall.portsOpen.  
   • Sort the port numbers in ascending order.  
   • Write them to /home/user/hardening/open_ports.txt so that the file format is:  
     port1  
     port2  
     …  
     portN  
     TOTAL: N  
     Example of perfect formatting when three ports exist:  
     22  
     80  
     443  
     TOTAL: 3  
   • Each line must end with a single Unix newline and there must be no blank trailing line after “TOTAL: N”.

3. **Generate a hardened configuration**  
   • Build a new JSON document named /home/user/hardening/config_hardened.json.  
   • Start with the original config.json but modify the firewall section so that the .portsOpen array **does not include 80**; keep only 22 and 443.  
   • Ensure "passwordAuthentication" remains "no".  
   • The resulting file must be pretty-printed JSON using two-space indentation and a trailing newline.

Only 3 – 4 shell commands (plus any small inline script if needed) should be enough. No root privileges are required.  
Make sure every requested output file is produced exactly with the specified paths and contents, because automated grading will compare them byte-for-byte.
name: task_000000_3cc4f258
